---
title: "New Paper: Mitigating PSD Drift in Zero-Latency GW Searches"
description: "Our new paper introduces the Dual Cutler-Vallisneri Corrections, a novel perturbative framework to eliminate sky-localization and timing biases caused by power spectral density (PSD) drift in real-time gravitational-wave pipelines."
date: "2026-03-11"
categories: [Research, LIGO, Multi-Messenger Astronomy, Paper]
tags: [Gravitational Waves, Data Analysis, General Relativity, Early Warning]
image: ../img/gtsp-c0/sky_impact_analysis.png
---

<div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 2em;">
  <a class="btn btn-primary" href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09132" role="button" target="_blank" style="margin-right: 10px;">
    <i class="bi bi-file-earmark-pdf"></i> Read Paper (ArXiv)
  </a>
  <a class="btn btn-outline-primary" href="https://git.ligo.org/james.kennington/zlw" role="button" target="_blank">
    <i class="bi bi-code-slash"></i> View Code
  </a>
</div>

## The Goal: Catching Mergers in Real Time

As the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA network pushes deeper into the universe, the goal isn't just to detect black holes and neutron stars after the fact—it's to catch them in *real time*. For multi-messenger astronomy, sending out zero-latency alerts before or exactly as a binary neutron star merges allows optical telescopes to catch the initial flash of a kilonova.

However, operating matched-filter pipelines at zero-latency introduces unique data analysis hurdles.

## The Challenge: PSD Drift and Metric Errors

To find signals, we filter the incoming strain data against a bank of theoretical templates. This process relies heavily on the detector's Power Spectral Density (PSD)—a measure of the background noise at different frequencies.

In an ideal world, the PSD is stationary. In reality, detector noise constantly fluctuates. Low-latency pipelines often have to use a "lagged" PSD to maintain computational efficiency and zero-latency throughput. This spectral drift induces severe systematic errors in the matched-filter metric. If uncorrected, this drift biases our timing estimates and drastically shifts the predicted sky-localization of the event, potentially sending telescopes looking in the wrong direction.

## The Solution: Dual Cutler-Vallisneri Corrections

To address this, we generalized the classic **Cutler-Vallisneri formalism**. Originally used to estimate parameter estimation errors due to waveform inaccuracies, we adapted it to handle metric errors caused by noise drift.

We developed a perturbative framework that calculates these expected metric shifts analytically. By applying our **Dual Cutler-Vallisneri Corrections**, we can dynamically adjust the matched-filter outputs. Crucially, these corrections are:
1. **Causal:** They don't require future data, preserving the zero-latency nature of the search.
2. **Computationally Efficient:** They add negligible overhead to the pipeline.

![Distributions of parameter biases before and after applying the corrections. The uncorrected drift shows significant systematic offsets, while our corrected framework restores the parameters to their true values.](../img/gtsp-c0/bias_distributions.png){#fig-biases width=60% fig-align="center"}

## The Result: Restoring Sky-Localization

We validated this framework empirically using real gravitational-wave strain data and PSDs from the GWTC-4.0 catalog, available via GWOSC. We generated numerical injections and tracked the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) drift over time.

![Tracking SNR and metric drift over time using experimental strain data.](../img/gtsp-c0/snr_drift.png){#fig-snr-drift width=50% fig-align="center"}

The impact on multi-messenger astronomy is immediate. By eliminating the timing and phase biases induced by PSD drift, we fully restore the accuracy of the early-warning sky maps.

![Impact on sky-localization. Our corrections dramatically shrink the error regions compared to the uncorrected zero-latency pipeline.](../img/gtsp-c0/sky_impact_analysis.png){#fig-sky-impact width=60% fig-align="center"}

This framework ensures that as we move into the next observing runs, our zero-latency alerts remain both lightning-fast and mathematically robust. Check out the full derivation and results in the paper [@kennington_dual_2026].

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
